reasoning: The ICE BofA AA US Corporate Index yield closed at a massive local peak of 5.08% on April 11, 2025, surging nearly 40 basis points in a week due to panic selling driven by sweeping global tariffs and the associated forced unwiding of Treasury basis swap trades. As of April 13, this 5.08% represents an extreme shock outlier. Historically, such violent spikes—particularly when they involve forced liquidations and prompt Federal Reserve reassurances (Fed President Susan Collins explicitly noted readiness to 'keep financial markets functioning')—are followed by mean reversion once the selling exhausts itself over the subsequent weeks. Thus, the probability of the yield holding above 5.08% is lowest in the short term as technical pressures abate. However, over the medium-to-long term, the structural inflationary impacts of the new tariffs provide some upward bias, meaning the yield could stabilize at a higher baseline. Aligning with the requirement to have uncertainty increase with the forecast horizon, the probabilities start well below 0.5 reflecting the expected short-term mean reversion, and scale closer to 0.50 as long-term macroeconomic volatility makes the distant outcomes harder to predict over a 3- to 6-month horizon.
